sentences.text: Mm-hm.
[SIGHS] Sad.
Sadness.
Lack of income.
I can't work.
Because I don't dare to say to anyone that I can give lessons in foreign languages.
Stalin's still alive.
Grishe has lost his job in the hospital because of me, works as a doctor in a factory, gets 70 rubles per month which is not enough to make your ends meet.
Plus our nanny's pension.
So we somehow we make ends meet, but we are very often hungry.
All of us live in one room.
Yeah.
And no friends, no relatives.
Grishe's relatives have also deserted us.
They're rich, don't want to hear anything from a person who has had a-- who has had a prison sentence or has been to prison for political reasons.
This was a Jewish attitude by the way.
Can't say anything nice about these people.
And later, little by little, little by little, we start living.
I get some pupils, become pregnant.
Naomi was born.
And after Naomi's birth there's a history of the Jewish doctors.
And we are already getting ready for Siberia again.
That was also a very hard time for my family, and Naomi was lying in her diapers.
I was called by a Russian woman whom I didn't know.
And she told-- she told me she was coming to pick me up.
Somebody wants to talk to me.
And I understood immediately it's from the KGB.
And I left my baby with my neighbor.
I phoned Grishe at work and I said [HEBREW].
Yeah.
He understood immediately.
And I was taken to a private house, where I was kept for hours by a Russian man, an investigator.
So he was a man who offered me-- if I don't want to go to prison again, he offered me very good salary to provide my family in Riga-- was a good salary-- to go to Sweden and to work for the KGB.
So I did a very clever thing that moment.
It lasted four or five hours, the conversation.
I was terribly upset and terribly nervous, and he saw it.
And the woman who had taken me to that place is a doctor from the KGB.
She wanted to see my reactions.
And I said, if you want me to get back to the place where I had been living with my baby, let's do the lunatic asylum.
You can send me by force, but I won't to do anything for you.
But that was also a terrible adventure for me.
So and such adventures repeatedly occurred until 1960, when I did an investigation, which lasted four months.
That was-- I believe that was the end of my common sense and of my ability to think.
But I was in Jewish life all the time.
No.
I was always frightened.
I was always afraid.
I was afraid of people.
There were only certain people who had access to my house because I didn't trust them.
And least of all, I trusted Soviet Jews who were living in Riga, because many of them were KGB agents.
Well, I had certain people.
I had some friends who were opera singers and musicians, artists, and who I knew exactly that they are my friends.
That's all I could rely on them, and I was-- I was right.
Which years?
Oh, yes.
So doing-- eh, for a, for a--
For a living, I gave lessons.
Then I gave lessons.
I was not afraid to give lessons, because the time had changed, you see?
The atmosphere had changed.
I was giving the French these English and German lessons without being frightened.
I was offered to tut-- teach at school at a institute.
And I said that I'm not going to teach at this--
The children grew up.
My children grew up in a very Zionist Jewish atmosphere on my side.
As you know, I have given my children Jewish names, Shulamit and Naomi, which nobody did in Riga those days.
I wanted them to be-- to remember their Jewish people.
I remember when Naomi was in the cradle, I sang to her.
I knew they will take me away again.
[HEBREW] I was sitting and crying bitterly.
And I thought, never ever will my child know she's Jewish, because she will have no mother.
And now you see she has a mother.
Again, in April.
Was the 26th of April, 1971, the happiest day.
Beyond description.
Absolutely.
Only when I saw Coca-Cola in Vienna.
I believed that I am-- that I am not in Russia.
Oh, yes.
I knew I would never be let out.
And I was told I would never be let out.
We got offered money.
We had to pay.
And I was happy there was somebody to whom I could pay.
They--
He and his wife [INAUDIBLE] took us to Moscow, and until we-- and we said goodbye to each other.
And we are in touch, what means-- He lives at-- he's an old man now.
He's 83 and very ill.
But he has a nice-- a nice home, and [INAUDIBLE] is a very good wife to him.
And we-- we correspond.
He knows that I will never, ever come to Riga.
He'd never suggest I come to Riga, and helping him financially as much as I can.
Yes, he did.
Yes, he came home very sick man.
He came directly to my house from the railway station.
Now and again, a postcard because he could write only once in six months.
He was not in jail.
He was in a hard labor camp, which is worse.
Everything.
He knew everything, yes.
I didn't divorce Vaboulis.
That was automatic as the KGB divorced people, political prisoners, as soon as they were arrested.
I was just-- a paper was read to me that on a certain date I was separated from him, and that's it.
Grishe I met-- I don't know when I met him.
I really don't know.
When Shulamit went to school, so because she needed a name.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
Oh, Grishe has been working as a doctor in many places-- in many places same time I mean.
First in [NON-ENGLISH] when we arrived and then in [NON-ENGLISH].
For 25 years he has been working.
And I was giving private lessons.
So I could help my children graduate from university.
I could help my grandchildren and still can help my grandchildren.
And I'm very happy about that.
Mm-hm.
Yeah.
Mm-hm.
I remember it exactly.
It was the night.
There was a heavy snowfall on the 8th of December, when I felt very restless hiding in Vaboulis' room.
He was not at home.
And later, it so happened that they found out that my family was killed, yeah?
And when I-- this was confirmed, I gave an oath to the dead that as long-- so long as I live, I will try to find them, to take them to court, to have them sentenced if possible.
And this is what I did.
I kept word.
So the first time you could do something was about this subject was in Sweden when you have your report?
No.
Not exactly.
I started with it in Riga when I was pregnant with Shulamit in 1948.
Shortly before I gave birth to her, I met in the street a Latvian man who was my neighbor in Goldingen and who led the killing of all Goldingen Jews, [INAUDIBLE].
I thought he was in Germany, but he was in Riga.
He jumped onto the train car.
I jumped after him, and I accompanied him.
He didn't notice me.
He got off.
I got off.
He went into the house.
I went after him.
I went to [INAUDIBLE], and she took me for a Latvian.
I said, you know, I know somewhere in this house is my friend [INAUDIBLE] must be living.
Perhaps you know where he lives?
And she told me where he lives, in her house.
And so I have a friend who was in the [INAUDIBLE].
It was a special organization who were looking for war criminals and for war crime-- punishing war crime, and I told him the story.
He's from Goldingen.
The man was not arrested.
He had-- he had been in prison in [INAUDIBLE] but he permits them to work as a KGB agent, and he was released.
And this was a very bad feeling, my first bad feeling about the war crime and the Russians.
They didn't care if Jews were killed or if they were not.
So started in-- in the full sense meaning of the word in-- in Sweden.
Here in Israel, of course.
That was 1976 when Arajs was arrested, when I went to the police department here for Nazi crime, department for Nazi crime and I told them my story.
And I remained in touch with them about the other-- about the rest of the-- of the gangsters.
And I gave them all the information I had.
Yes.
In the first place.
Evidence.
That was farther to the [INAUDIBLE] countries.
They-- they informed Germany about Arajs.
I am summoned to-- to go to-- on the 5th of July to go to Hamburg--
5th of May to Hamburg.
And I go there, Naomi accompanies me.
We're spending there 10 days. 10 days, I'm testifying there against him.
I'm sitting in the same courtroom he is sitting.
He's acting as a sick man, and he's not.
He's taken very good care of.
He keeps quiet all the time.
He doesn't speak to me.
There is no eye contact between us.
And I'm asked-- I'm not asked questions.
They didn't-- The jury didn't ask me questions in the beginning.
They had me tell my story, same story I'm telling you now, more or less, I'm telling them.
And then only, they start asking me questions.
And so everything seems to be correct.
A moment comes when the presiding judge, Dr.
Wagner, says-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
The presiding judge, Dr.
Wagner.
Yes.
W-A-G-N-E-R.
[SIGHS] So I'm saying my [NON-ENGLISH], my sister's photo.
I put on my glasses.
Go ahead.
Pointing at him.
That's him.
Just a-- a small photo.
And so the photo they showed me was very big.
It's about 30 people were sitting, uniformed people were [INAUDIBLE] all wearing the same uniform, and he was among them.
I don't know.
I was not looking at him.
He was not sitting across from me.
He was sitting the same line, you see.
A usual courtrooms as we see on television.
I would say the buildings are like in Riga.
And the-- the interiors are also a very usual courtroom.
Security was-- was strict on-- on entering.
There were some German girls standing, and they were checking our handbags and from top to toe.
And that's all.
In a Norwegian-- at [NON-ENGLISH] A Norwegian hotel, yes, [NON-ENGLISH].
In Germany?
I felt nothing absolutely, because I had no-- no contact with the-- with the outer world.
In the hotel, people were very friendly.
They knew we had come for a trial.
And the jury was very friendly.
No.
No, no, no.
No.
Pro, yes.
I don't even know whether I should mention it.
There were two-- two trials here in Israel later, because the [NON-ENGLISH] were not pleased with the outcome of the trial.
Lother Klemm.
Yes.
L-O-T-H-E-R, Klemm, K-L-E-M-M.
A youngish man.
Very friendly, very candid, trying to help.
Very sincere contact.
Mr.
Fritz Steinaecker.
Yes.
F-R-I-T-Z.
Steinaecker, S-T-E-I-N-A-E-C-K-E-R.
Steinaecker.
From Frankfurt he was.
Very, very capable man.
Very capable man.
He was correct.
He was polite as all Germans are.
I didn't-- I didn't have the feeling that he was trying to do me any harm in that.
He just wanted to earn his money, that's all.
When he came-- when he-- On one occasion, he thought, he felt that he had lost the case.
When Arajs-- he said-- at the end-- the end of the trial, but how did you know that what was done to you was done by Arajs?
So I said, he said to me, you bitch.
Do you know who's standing before you?
I'm Viktors Arajs.
He said, [GERMAN], I have no more questions.
And that was the end.
But here in Israel during the interval, he came up to me and said, Mrs.
Hait, why are you so hostile to me?
I said, yes I am.
But why?
I said, it's either ideologically or financially interested that you tried to set a war criminal free.
And in both cases, it's appalling.
He didn't say a word.
That was the last time you spoke to him?
Yes.
I hope so.
Put them here on the table, the names.
[INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE] or something like that.
No, not at all.
I didn't feel-- in America, security was very strict and very reliable.
In Germany, I did not feel any security at all.
Perhaps there was one, but we were not aware of it.
We were also-- we were not told that there is security.
For instance, once-- on one occasion, we were downstairs, we saw an old man sit there as if drunk.
So I said-- no, Naomi said to me, so this may be a Latvian, you know, just watching us.
Mm-hm.
I don't remember exactly-- no, there was one session a day only.
One session a day, yes.
In German.
I prefer German, because I didn't want any translator and it was not needed.
And I think they were also satisfied because makes things easier.
Uh, I knew I have to be strong, I knew I have to do my job.
I knew that it has to be proven that this man is the greatest murderer of eastern Europe during the war, that's he responsible for the killing of Latvian Jewry and he has to spend his life in prison.
Yes.
In the first place, I didn't take her with me.
She suggested to come with me in order to protect me and to help me.
Like me, I believe, she helped me a lot.
I wouldn't have been able to do the job without that moral help.
One of my biggest impressions is the moment I went up to identify the man.
Second one to answer the question by Steinaecker, why I thought it was Arajs.
And there were actually no other special incidents that are to be remembered.
No.
That was the worst part of the trial.
They had not published-- that the German authorities that a war-- an infamous war criminal is going to be punished.
No publicity at all.
We were all alone there.
A Latvian woman over there.
A Latvian woman.
We don't know, but she was a person who was interested to find out the truth.
And then there was a Jewish family and a husband, wife, and son.
I don't know their name anymore.
Their son was Benny, who had served in Israel in [INAUDIBLE] unit.
But since the lady herself and her husband are victims of the Holocaust and she had been sterilized, they were afraid to lose their son, so he came back.
They were forthcoming and friendly before the trial also.
They were standing in the corridor waiting for us.
They were there.
And there was a correspondent who later wrote an article.
Yes.
10 days after we had arrived, so it must have been on the 14th I believe or the 15th.
Yes.
Yom Ha'atzmaut, Independence Day.
Can't speak about it.
It hurts.
We had a lot of time off, but we spent it in the hotel room.
Well, just--
--sleeping, talking, reading, nothing special.
For the trial?
Yes, I did.
A lot of homework to do.
Trying to recall facts of the past, trying to recall not only Arajs and his deeds, but people associated with all these terrible acts and dates and different moments.
I did it at night.
I did it always alone and at night.
No.
No.
There are no local Hamburg Jews anymore.
There are Jews from Iran selling carpets, so what have they got to do with the Holocaust?
[SIGHS] Satisfaction.
Moral satisfaction.
My duty carried out.
Mm-hm.
We don't know anything about it.
Only 1979, I get a phone call from our local police telling me that [NON-ENGLISH] are displeased with the trial.
They want a second trial, and they suggest I come to Germany on a certain date.
That was by phone.
I refused immediately and said categorically I have got nothing new to tell them.
Whatever I had, I have told them.
Tell the gentlemen I have an address in Bat-Yam so and so, and they can come and visit me.
And they did.
There was a trial in-- in Tel Aviv, in [NON-ENGLISH] near the museum.
What do you mean?
No.
I was-- they came to me because of me, not-- there was not a trial.
It was an investigation and a cross-examination.
But Steinaecker was here, the lawyer, and et cetera, et cetera.
He died in prison after 10 years of imprisonment.
He got-- since there is no capital punishment, he got a life sentence.
In prison.
My feelings towards the Germans have not changed.
Absolutely.
A trial can't change my feelings towards them.
I find-- I find them guilty for crime against humanity, particularly against my people.
And when I saw now on television the [NON-ENGLISH] in Frankfurt, it only proves that I am right despite all the-- all the sorts of my closest friends who say that there's a different Germany now.
Good.
